  :                            304 
  :                            305 				. the stack of four-word task descriptors
  :                            306 				. grows downwards from the position below

  :                            308

  :                            326 				. *next points to next_descriptor
  :                            327 				. for small banks
  :                            328 				. this is the next available two-word
  :                            329 				. descriptor in the upward-growing
  :                            330 				. small banks decriptor list
  :                            331 
  :                            332 				. *next and the initial descriptor
  :                            333 				. where *next points are constructed
  :                            334 				. here at system start, so all
  :                            335 				. system images are restartable
  :                            336 				. and may be in ROM
  :                            337 
  :                            338 				. The buffer pointer in the initial
  :                            339 				. *next descriptor  points to the
  :                            340 				. first available RAM page after
  :                            341 				. the kernel ROM or kernel load
  :                            342 
  :                            343 				. there are 64 free-chains into the
  :                            344 				. "small" banks heap and one free-chain 
  :                            345 				. into the big-banks stack
  :                            346 	
  :                            347 				. The small-banks free-chains are one
  :                            348 				. per size, 1..64 pages (4096 words
  :                            349 				. to 262144 words)
  :                            350 
  :                            351 				. a "small" bank of 262144 words is
  :                            352 				. possible, because a smaller bank
  :                            353 				. may get bigger if the next assignment
  :                            354 				. does not fit in the same 262144-word
  :                            355 				. physical bank
  :                            356 
  :                            357 				. small banks are assigned from low-order
  :                            358 				. locations upwards. The free chains
  :                            359 				. reassign free storage at the lowest
  :                            360 				. addresses first.
  :                            361 
  :                            362 				. Freed space above the highest
  :                            363 				. assigned small bank is recombined
  :                            364 				. as single free area between the
  :                            365 				. the stack of small banks which grows
  :                            366 				. upwards and compresses downwards, and
  :                            367 				. the stack of big banks, which grows
  :                            368 				. downwards and compresses upwards
  :                            369 
  :                            370 				. There is one large-banks free-chain
  :                            371 				. for all sizes. A big bank may combine
  :                            372 				. one to nearly 65536 adjacent physical
  :                            373 				. banks of 262144 words
  :                            374 
  :                            375 				. A single large bank may contain nearly
  :                            376 				. 1024 times the 16 Megaword Address space
  :                            377 				.          -16 gigawords in total-
  :                            378 				. for large arrays which need to be contiguous
  :                            379 
  :                            380 				. An array needs to be contiguous if
  :                            381 				. multiword operands to single instructions
  :                            382 				. straddle physical 256K bank boundaries.
  :                            383 
  :                            384 				. Developers can also structure arrays
  :                            385 				. so that quad or double word operands
  :                            386 				. are safely inside one bank, even if
  :                            387 				. the containing structure straddles two
  :                            388 				. non-contiguous banks addressed with
  :                            389 				. two adjacent relocation base-registers
  :                            390 
  :                            391 				. Operand EA is translated to an absolute
  :                            392 				. address once for each operand read or write
  :                            393 
  :                            394 				. However very large contiguous bank
  :                            395 				. arrays are obtained with one ARRAY$ call
  :                            396 				. and accessed with one bank handle
  :                            397 
  :                            398 				. Arrays larger than the address space
  :                            399 				. are addressed a part at
  :                            400 				. a time. Structures straddling bank boundaries
  :                            401 				. are accessed using two adjacent base registers
  :                            402 				. to address two adjacent banks
  :                            403 
  :                            404 	.

  :                            413 .	these are here to read or write
  :                            414 .	a pointer in the kernel vector bank
  :                            415 
  :                            416 .	from routines which might be called
  :                            417 .	from either a kernel task or an

  :                            421 .	visible instead of the application

  :                            425 .	whichever it was before

  :                            9 .    RTA1 is a free processor architecture specification.
  :                            10 .
  :                            11 .    The executable emulation of RTA1 is free software.
  :                            12 .
  :                            13 .    This instruction code for the target RTA1 architecture is free software
  :                            14 .
  :                            15 .    Software programs delivered with this software to connect the
  :                            16 .    emulated RTA1 with real network interfaces in the emulator host
  :                            17 .    are free software
  :                            18 .
  :                            19 .    Scripts and programs delivered with this software for running
  :                            20 .    on other computers and interacting with the RTA1 are free software
  :                            21 .
  :                            22 .    Scripts and utility programs for constructing RTA1 target
  :                            23 .    executable software are free software

  :                            27 .    the Free Software Foundation, either version 3 of the License, or

  :                            42 ?	$do	p(),	call	(p(?, 1))
  :                            43 	$end

                                					calibration	;

                                					agent1		;
  :                            50 					agent2
  :                            51 	$endif

  :                            237 .	the kernel vector bank is read and
  :                            238 .	written with internal interrupts
  :                            239

  :                            246 .	processes which have their own vector
  :                            247 .	bank
  :                            248

  :                            251

  :                            253

  :                            1 .       
  :                            2 .    Copyright Tim Cox, 2012
  :                            3 .    TimMilesCox@gmx.ch
  :                            4 .
  :                            5 .    This file is instruction code for the freeware processor architecture
  :                            6 .
  :                            7 .                RTA1
  :                            8 .
  :                            9 .    RTA1 is a free processor architecture specification.
  :                            10 .
  :                            11 .    The executable emulation of RTA1 is free software.
  :                            12 .
  :                            13 .    This instruction code for the target RTA1 architecture is free software
  :                            14 .
  :                            15 .    Software programs delivered with this software to connect the
  :                            16 .    emulated RTA1 with real network interfaces in the emulator host
  :                            17 .    are free software
  :                            18 .
  :                            19 .    Scripts and programs delivered with this software for running
  :                            20 .    on other computers and interacting with the RTA1 are free software
  :                            21 .
  :                            22 .    Scripts and utility programs for constructing RTA1 target
  :                            23 .    executable software are free software      
  :                            24 .
  :                            25 .    You can redistribute it and/or modify RTA1
  :                            26 .    under the terms of the GNU General Public License as published by
  :                            27 .    the Free Software Foundation, either version 3 of the License, or
  :                            28 .    (at your option) any later version.
  :                            29 .
  :                            30 .    RTA1 is distributed in the hope that it will be useful,
  :                            31 .    but WITHOUT ANY WARRANTY; without even the implied warranty of
  :                            32 .    MERCHANTABILITY or FITNESS FOR A PARTICULAR PURPOSE.  See the
  :                            33 .    GNU General Public License for more details.
  :                            34 .
  :                            35 .    You should have received a copy of the GNU General Public License
  :                            36 .    along with RTA1.  If not, see <http://www.gnu.org/licenses/>.
  :                            37 .
  :                            38 .
  :                            39 
  :                            40 . Emulation implements one kind of network interface
  :                            41 
  :                            42 . this can be modified when there is more than one interface type
  :                            43 . initially the only known type is modelled as a dual ported memory
  :                            44 . interface to the system
  :                            45 .
  :                            46 . half of the interfacing memory presents input frames
  :                            47 . the other half is available for output frames
  :                            48 
  :                            49 
  :                            50 .		_________________________________
  :                            51 .	>------>|1st receive buffer		|
  :                            52 .	|	|2nd				|
  :                            53 .	|	|				|
  :                            54 .	^
  :                            55 .	|
  :                            56 .	<-------|last receive buffer		|
  :                            57 .		|_______________________________|
  :                            58 .	>------>|1st transmit buffer		|
  :                            59 .	|	|2nd				|
  :                            60 .	|	|				|
  :                            61 .	^
  :                            62 .	|
  :                            63 .	<-------|last transmit buffer		|
  :                            64 .		|_______________________________|
  :                            65 
  :                            66 
  :                            67 . the frames arrive and are sent in cyclic order of the buffers
  :                            68 .
  :                            69 . there is an 8-word length + ethernet header at the start of each buffer
  :                            70 . this sketch represents a quartet of bits per symbol
  :                            71 
  :                            72 .	 00LLLL 00xxxx 00xxxx 00xxxx 00yyyy 00yyyy 00yyyy 00pppp
  :                            73 
  :                            74 .	________________________________________________________________________
  :                            75 .	| 00LLLL | 00xxxx | 00xxxx | 00xxxx | 00yyyy | 00yyyy | 00yyyy | 00pppp|
  :                            76 .	|________|________|________|________|________|________|________|_______|
  :                            77 .	| 00data | 00....
  :                            78 
  :                            79 
  :                            80 . network data is buffered 16 bits per computer word right up to the socket
  :                            81 . and XDR interfaces. These "interface dual ported" memories may be 16-bit
  :                            82 . memories reading out eight zero bits at the high order positions of each
  :                            83 . computer word, or they may be 24-bit memories: then the high-order 8 bits
  :                            84 . must be maintained with zero values
  :                            85 
  :                            86 .	LLLL	is frame size, and nonzero value written here indicates
  :                            87 .		a complete frame received, or available for transmission
  :                            88 .
  :                            89 .	xxxx xxxx xxxx	is the destination ethernet address
  :                            90 
  :                            91 .	yyyy yyyy yyyy	is the source ethernet address
  :                            92 
  :                            93 .	pppp	is the protocol family indicator, 0800 for IPv4, 0806 for ARP
  :                            94 .		as many as required
  :                            95 
  :                            96 
  :                            97 . the buffers are equal sized and used cyclically
  :                            98 
  :                            99 . the input machine signals to software that a frame is available
  :                            100 . by writing a length in bits 15:0 of the first word in the buffer:
  :                            101 .
  :                            102 .		0000 0000 LLLL LLLL LLLL LLLL
  :                            103 .
  :                            104 . The length is not self-inclusive. It is the frame length.
  :                            105 . The frame starts at the next word
  :                            106 
  :                            107 . software indicates that the frame has been sampled and the buffer is
  :                            108 . free by writing zero to this length word
  :                            109 
  :                            110 . software indicates that a frame for transmission is assembled 
  :                            111 . in the transmission buffer by writing a length in bits 15:0 of
  :                            112 . the first word in the transmission buffer. The length is the
  :                            113 . frame length and not self-inclusive
  :                            114 
  :                            115 . The frame for transmission starts in the second word of the
  :                            116 . transmission buffer
  :                            117 
  :                            118 . Transmission completion is indicated back to the software by zero
  :                            119 . in the length word, and this also indicates the transmission buffer
  :                            120 . is again free
  :                            121 
  :                            122 . This interface model is not part of RTA architecture but supports
  :                            123 . this emulation and is intended to work like network interfaces are 
  :                            124 . intended for RTA systems
  :                            125 . 
  :                            126 . That is, some of the 63 DEVICEs (arrays outside system memory)
  :                            127 . should carry network data to and from an RTA system.
  :                            128 
  :                            129 . A DEVICE is up to 65536 BANKS of up to 262144 WORDS of up to 24 bits
  :                            130 .
  :                            131 
  :                            132 . device 0 is system memory or executable space and is up to 16 gigawords
  :                            133 . of 24-bit memory
  :                            134 
  :                            135 . only system memory is selectable in pages of 4096 words as well as in
  :                            136 . banks of 256K
  :                            137 
  :                            138 . Memory in the other 63 possible devices is selectable in banks of
  :                            139 . 262144 words only
  :                            140 
  :                            141 . the pointer to a page of system memory based in one of the 72 relocation
  :                            142 . registers has the layout
  :                            143 
  :                            144 .	00pp pppp pppp pppp pppp pppp
  :                            145 
  :                            146 . where bit 22 = 0 indicates a 22-bit page pointer and the task has a granule
  :                            147 . of 4096 words addressed and available
  :                            148 
  :                            149 . the pointer to a bank of system memory in a relocation register has the layout
  :                            150 
  :                            151 .	01pp pppp pppp pppp pp00 0000
  :                            152 
  :                            153 . where bit 22 = 1 indicates a 16-bit bank pointer and the task has a granule
  :                            154 . of 262144 words addressed and available
  :                            155 
  :                            156 . bits 5:0 = 00 0000 indicate that the bank is in system memory
  :                            157 .
  :                            158 . the pointer to a bank of "I/O Memory" (strictly an array other than
  :                            159 . system memory) has the layout in a relocation register
  :                            160 
  :                            161 .	01bb bbbb bbbb bbbb bbDE VICE
  :                            162 
  :                            163 . where bit 22 = 1 = Big Bank ** DEVICE = nonzero = some other device than
  :                            164 . system memory
  :                            165 
  :                            166 . Some of these 63 possible arrays outside system memory will be battery-
  :                            167 . supported SRAMS of 24-bit words containing realtime transaction file systems
  :                            168 
  :                            169 . Others may be flash memories containing default system images and occasional
  :                            170 . data backups (but flash memories may not be used for high-frequency
  :                            171 . transaction update data)
  :                            172 
  :                            173 . some may be insertable flashes containing 16- or 8-bit memories organised
  :                            174 . in a file system architecture readable and writable by other systems
  :                            175 
  :                            176 . some may be video RAMs of up to 16 Gigawords of 24-bit words
  :                            177 
  :                            178 . At least one device must be an array interfacing a network adaptor to the
  :                            179 . RTA system with 16 network data bits per computer word as modelled here
  :                            180 
  :                            181 . This interface array is supplied to the emulator by a Unix shared memory
  :                            182 . block of 524288 bytes which looks to RTA like a device of one bank of
  :                            183 . 262144 16-bit words.
  :                            184 
  :                            185 . RTA reads each word out as a 24-bit computer word with bits 23:16 = 0000 0000
  :                            186 . The first half of this block is receive buffers and the second half transmit
  :                            187 . buffers
  :                            188 
  :                            189 . the settings here are that RPAGEW is 1024 words. This is the buffer size
  :                            190 . and it allows an ethernet frame of 1514+ bytes. Jumbos are not accounted
  :                            191 . for in the emulation. A device in a large-configuration RTA may have
  :                            192 . 65536 * 262144 words in a network interface device, but the emulator has
  :                            193 . only 1 * 262144 words in its one network interface space.
  :                            194 
  :                            195 . Therefore the emulator will only test standard ethernet traffic
  :                            196 
  :                            197 . the configuration of the emulated interface (which links to a real interface
  :                            198 . outside the emulator) is therefore
  :                            199 
  :                            200 .	128 receive buffers of 1024 words each
  :                            201 . plus	128 transmit buffers of 1024 words each
  :                            202 
  :                            203 
  :                            204 . Notes. Note 1
  :                            205 
  :                            206 . To avoid accidental damage, device banks (as distinct from system memory
  :                            207 . banks) may only be based by interrupt code. This avoids inadvertant
  :                            208 . traversal of array accesses across all the devices. At its simplest,
  :                            209 . the internal interrupts (system calls II 64+32 .. II 64+63) only need
  :                            210 . to copy the bank handle from user-A register, base it, and return:
  :                            211 
  :                            212 .		la	A
  :                            213 .		sabr	32
  :                            214 .		ir	0
  :                            215 
  :                            216 		.
  :                            217 		.
  :                            218 
  :                            219 .		la	A
  :                            220 .		sabr	63
  :                            221 .		ir	0
  :                            222 
  :                            223 . but as much extra security can be added to that as wished in more complex OS
  :                            224 
  :                            225 . Once the storage area is based, the application can access it at will.
  :                            226 . If this is not wished, the interrupt code itself must carry out required
  :                            227 . operations and unbase the storage area before return. The simplest model
  :                            228 . here relies on trusted applications which base the sensitive banks
  :                            229 . by design and not by accident
  :                            230 
  :                            231 . This also provides the beginning of a security model for any implementation
  :                            232 . which includes the translatable storage handle
  :                            233 
  :                            234 . Where a handle 0xxx xxxx xxxx xxxx xxxx is absolute whether it points
  :                            235 . to system memory or a device
  :                            236 
  :                            237 . 1xxx xxxx xxxx xxxx xxxx is reserved for a possible translating range of
  :                            238 . storage area handles, for moveable/swappable application storage.
  :                            239 . A hardware implementation which does translatable handles would reserve
  :                            240 . the basing of all absolute areas (device and system memory) to privileged
  :                            241 . code of some sort, perhaps system calls as in this initial model
  :                            242 
  :                            243 . applications could then only directly base an allowed set of areas described
  :                            244 . in their translate lists.
  :                            245 
  :                            246 
  :                            247 
  :                            248 . Note 2
  :                            249 
  :                            250 . the interface handler here will offer each frame to the protocol family
  :                            251 . (mostly IPv4 or similar), which must decide immediately whether to take
  :                            252 . the frame and place it in storage visible to the application.
  :                            253 
  :                            254 . This means IP will check IP correctness, and call ICMP, UDP or TCP which
  :                            255 . must checksum as necessary while copying the datagram to socket storage.
  :                            256 
  :                            257 . This is not a flat-address system and there is no zero-copy
  :                            258 . receive. But there is only one copy, and it is carried out during checksum
  :                            259 . generation. After that the datagrams are in socket-associated buffers which
  :                            260 . are placed in the address space of the application.
  :                            261 
  :                            262 . The socket or XDR interface will format the data when the application
  :                            263 . subsequently receives it over the socket API.
  :                            264 
  :                            265 . on return from the call to the protocol family, the interface buffer is
  :                            266 . freed
  :                            267 
  :                            268 
  :                            269 
  :                            270 RX_LL_HL $equ	d$save
  :                            271 
  :                            272 
  :                            273 
  :                            274 $(1)
  :                            275 
  :                            276 .	pointer array to the network interface descriptors
  :                            277 .	these are numbered starting from one as in MIB-II
  :                            278 .	interfaces table
  :                            279 
  :                            280 .	word zero of this table is a count of the number of
  :                            281 .	interface descriptors in use
  :                            282 
  :                            283 .	the initial value 1 indicates that there is one
  :                            284 .	interface so far, interface(1) = internal loopback,
  :                            285 .	and the next to be configured ought to be interface(2)
  :                            286 
  :                            287 .	to obtain a descriptor for a new interface, increment
  :                            288 .	the counter in the first word of the array and select
  :                            289 .	the pointer at
  :                            290 
  :                            291 .	i_f_pointer_array[i_f_pointer_array[0]]
  :                            292 
  :                            293 .	for example
  :                            294 .
  :                            295 .		lx		i_f_pointer_array
  :                            296 .		ax, xi		1
  :                            297 .		sx		i_f_pointer_array
  :                            298 .		lx		0, x
  :                            299 
  :                            300 
  :                            301 i_f_pointer_array	. who wants to go multiplying subscripts?

  :                            56 net_bank
  :                            57 	inA	device$flags
  :                            58 	aa, xi	1
  :                            59 	anu	(SCALE_THRESHOLD)
  :                            60 
  :                            61 	jc	large_netbank
  :                            62 
  :                            63 small_netbank
  :                            64 	la	(4096*NET_BANKS)
  :                            65 	ii	BANK$
  :                            66 	jza	emergency_netbank
  :                            67 
  :                            68 	lx	(NET_BASE*/12)
  :                            69 .	sx	rx_buffers
  :                            70 
  :                            71 ?	$do	NET_BANKS,	base_netpage
  :                            72 
  :                            73 	la	(4096*RX_BANKS)
  :                            74 	lcal	init_pool
  :                            75 
  :                            76 	lx	(NET_BASE+RX_BANKS*/12)
  :                            77 .	sx	tx_buffers
  :                            78 	la	(4096*TX_BANKS)
  :                            79 	lcal	init_pool
  :                            80 
  :                            81 	lret	0
  :                            82 
  :                            83 large_netbank
  :                            84 	la	(262144*NET_BANKS)
  :                            85 	ii	BANK$
  :                            86 	jza	small_netbank
  :                            87 
  :                            88 	lx	(NET_BASE*/18:s)
  :                            89 .	sx	rx_buffers
  :                            90 
  :                            91 ?	$do	NET_BANKS,	base_netbank
  :                            92 
  :                            93 	la	(262144*RX_BANKS)
  :                            94 	lcal	init_pool
  :                            95 
  :                            96 	lx	(NET_BASE+RX_BANKS*/18:s)
  :                            97 .	sx	tx_buffers
  :                            98 	la	(262144*TX_BANKS)
  :                            99 	lcal	init_pool
  :                            100 
  :                            101 	lret	0
  :                            102 
  :                            103 emergency_netbank
  :                            104 
  :                            105 	.	can we get 16K then?
  :                            106 
  :                            107 	la, xi	4096*4
  :                            108 	ii	BANK$
  :                            109 	jza	minimal_emergency_netbank
  :                            110 
  :                            111 	lx	(NET_BASE*/12:s)
  :                            112 .	sx	rx_buffers
  :                            113 
  :                            114 ?	$do	3,	base_netpage
  :                            115 	sabr	NET_BASE+RX_BANKS	. keep 60*/18 as a reliable
  :                            116 					. pointer to transmission buffers
  :                            117 	la, xi	3*4096
  :                            118 	lcal	init_pool
  :                            119 
  :                            120 	lx	(NET_BASE+RX_BANKS*/12)
  :                            121 .	sx	tx_buffers
  :                            122 
  :                            123 	la, xi	4096
  :                            124 	lcal	init_pool
  :                            125 	lret	0
  :                            126 
  :                            127 
  :                            128 minimal_emergency_netbank
  :                            129 
  :                            130 	.	can we get 8K then?
  :                            131 
  :                            132 	la, xi	4096*2
  :                            133 	ii	BANK$
  :                            134 	jza	nowhere_to_go_netbank
  :                            135 
  :                            136 	lx	(NET_BASE*/12:s)
  :                            137 .	sx	rx_buffers
  :                            138 
  :                            139 	base_netpage
  :                            140 	sabr	NET_BASE+RX_BANKS
  :                            141 
  :                            142 	la, xi	4096
  :                            143 	lcal	init_pool
  :                            144 
  :                            145 	lx	(NET_BASE+RX_BANKS*/12)
  :                            146 .	sx	tx_buffers
  :                            147 
  :                            148 	la, xi	4096
  :                            149 	lcal	init_pool
  :                            150 	lret	0
  :                            151 
  :                            152 
  :                            153 nowhere_to_go_netbank
  :                            154 
  :                            155 .	if we couldn't get 8K for network buffers
  :                            156 .	then we won't start any new tasks either
  :                            157 
  :                            158 .	we should maybe set an LED pattern
  :                            159 .	and exit the IP process
  :                            160 
  :                            161 .	otherwise
  :                            162 .	we could program a flash to say it's always full
  :                            163 .	and no buffer is ever available in it
  :                            164 
  :                            165 	lret	0
  :                            166

  :                            40 HEADER_WORDS	$equ	d$save	. 16 used in receive
  :                            41 HEADER_LONGWORDS $equ	d$save1	. 17 used in receive
  :                            42 TP_HEADER	$equ	d$save2	. 18 used in receive and transmit
  :                            43 I_F_IDX		$equ	d$save3	. 19 used in receive
  :                            44 
  :                            45 I_F_P		$equ	8	. used in receive
  :                            46 TX_AXW		$equ	8	. used in transmit
  :                            47 TX_FRAME	$equ	9	. used in receive and transmit
  :                            48 
  :                            49 HEADER_BYTES	$equ	d$save	. 16 used in transmit
  :                            50 MTU		$equ	d$save1	. 17 used in transmit
  :                            51 I_F		$equ	d$save2	. 19 used in transmit
  :                            52 
  :                            53 TX_FLAG_OFFSET	$equ	10	. used in tranmsit
  :                            54 TX_DATAGRAML	$equ	11	. used in transmit = unfragmented datagram
  :                            55 				. generated in interfaces = xmitted frame
  :                            56 
  :                            57 DYNAMIC_IP_HEADER $equ	0
  :                            58 		. if IP header length cannot be known at socket layer
  :                            59 		. then make this a multiple of 4 > 20 and < 64
  :                            60 		. to give a fixed large space for IP to add header
  :                            61 		. options
  :                            62 
  :                            63 		. otherwise IP header size is fixed when the socket
  :                            64 		. layer acquires a buffer
  :                            65

  :                            311 .	ipv4_rx, input driver for a system with fixed dual ported
  :                            312 .	interface buffers
  :                            313 
  :                            314 .	this is an upward call from routine frames
  :                            315 
  :                            316 .	copying to socket-visible buffers is combined with
  :                            317 .	checksumming
  :                            318 
  :                            319 .	each word in the interface buffers is read only once
  :                            320 
  :                            321 .	ipv4_rx pushes and checks the IP headers in the
  :                            322 .	internal stack, which is a register array
  :                            323 
  :                            324 .	upper protocols obtain a buffer, then pop the IP headers
  :                            325 .	into it ahead of their own protocol message for
  :                            326 .	checksumming during transfer

  :                            91 udp4_x
00:0003BC 350000              +92 	lret	0
  :                            93 
  :                            94 
  :                            95 .	this is the direct feed to udpq from interfaces which have
  :                            96 .	fixed dual-ported buffering. Interfaces with a direct access
  :                            97 .	to system memory would not have this packet copy
  :                            98 
  :                            99 .	The packet copy is used as an occasion to checksum
  :                            100 
  :                            101 .	The packet copy places the packets in rx_buffer with
  :                            102 .	T77 descriptors which are called ACWs
  :                            103 
  :                            104 .	This routine cannot be called for fragmented datagrams
  :                            105 .	because it is instant from frame readout to UDP checksum
  :                            106 .	generation
  :                            107 
  :                            108 .	this is an upward call from ipv4_rx
  :                            109 
00:0003BD 580012              +110 udp_rx	ly	TP_HEADER
  :                            111 
00:0003BE FD0001              +112 	MIB_ICOUNT48	udpInDatagrams:i
(+01)0f
00:0003BF 2F0426              +112 
00:0003C0 5E03C2              +112 
(+01)0f
00:0003C1 170425              +112 
  :                            113 
00:0003C2 8FB000              +114 	qpush	0, y
00:0003C3 8D0004              +115 	ay, xi	4
00:0003C4 60E003              +116 	la	descriptor:bytes
00:0003C5 D0F002              +117 	ana	uugram:udgraml
00:0003C6 5E026C              +118 	jnc	ipv4_drop
  :                            119 
00:0003C7 CFF002              +120 	dl	uugram:udgraml	. length:checksum
00:0003C8 C5FFF8              +121 	aa, xi	-8
00:0003C9 5E026C              +122 	jnc	ipv4_drop
00:0003CA 20E003              +123 	sa	descriptor:bytes
00:0003CB DE03D4              +124 	jnzb	udp_rx_check
  :                            125 
00:0003CC 87A000              +126 	qpop	0, x
00:0003CD 850004              +127 	ax, xi	4
00:0003CE 10E006              +128 	sx	descriptor:apdata
  :                            129 
00:0003CF 3E047D              +130 	lcal	rx_nocheck
  :                            131 
(+01)10
00:0003D0 5D0301              +132 	ly, xi	udpq
00:0003D1 60000E              +133 	la	fp		. the descriptor
00:0003D2 3E0C50              +134 	lcal	q2
00:0003D3 B6026F              +135 	j	ipv4_rxz
  :                            136 
  :                            137 .	this is a protocol which has a length in the header
  :                            138 .	so the first step is to see if the IP datagram is
  :                            139 .	long enough to contain the UDP datagram
  :                            140 .	after that the operative length is the one in
  :                            141 .	the header
  :                            142 
  :                            143 udp_rx_check
00:0003D4 C70010              +144 	ds	LAST_BYTES	. LAST_BYTES:CHECKSUM
  :                            145 
00:0003D5 400004              +146 	lr	a
00:0003D6 CF0006              +147 	dl	6
00:0003D7 D7F000              +148 	da	uugram:sourcep
00:0003D8 C0F002              +149 	aa	uugram:udgraml
00:0003D9 C70006              +150 	ds	6
00:0003DA 600000              +151 	la	r
  :                            152 
00:0003DB 87A000              +153 	qpop	0, x
00:0003DC 850004              +154 	ax, xi	4
00:0003DD 10E006              +155 	sx	descriptor:apdata
  :                            156 
00:0003DE 3E0479              +157 	lcal	udp_rx_payload
  :                            158 
00:0003DF D00011              +159 	ana	CHECKSUM
00:0003E0 D6026C              +160 	jnza	ipv4_drop
  :                            161 
(+01)10
00:0003E1 5D0301              +162 	ly, xi	udpq
00:0003E2 60000E              +163 	la	fp
00:0003E3 3E0C50              +164 	lcal	q2
  :                            165 	
00:0003E4 B6026F              +166 	j	ipv4_rxz
  :                            167 
  :                            168 
  :                            169 .	this is the feed to udpq for reassembled datagrams
  :                            170 .	which are already in a chain of rx_buffer with ACW
  :                            171 .	descriptors
  :                            172 
  :                            173 .	each fragment has the checksum addition of its data
  :                            174 .	in the ACW, to exploit the chance of checksum
  :                            175 .	generation during copy to rx_buffer
  :                            176 
  :                            177 .	it has been second-guessed in all cases that checksum
  :                            178 .	is specified in the UDP header (the UDP header may not
  :                            179 .	be the first fragment of the datagram to arrive).
  :                            180 
  :                            181 .	also, it is unlikely that UDP payload large enough
  :                            182 .	to need reassembly would be exempt from checksum
  :                            183 
  :                            184 .	no-checksum is usually a feature of high-speed
  :                            185 .	media traffic which is transmitted in much smaller
  :                            186 .	packets than any MTU
  :                            187 
  :                            188 .	large reassembly is more likely to be traffic like
  :                            189 .	NFS in 8KByte slabs (or 3KByte slabs where RTA is the
  :                            190 .	NFS client). Checksum would certainly apply
  :                            191 
  :                            192 .	So all the data arriving here has been end-around
  :                            193 .	added and the positive sum left in the ACW
  :                            194 
  :                            195 .	if the UDP header has a nonzero checksum, the data sums
  :                            196 .	are now summed with network address, protocol index
  :                            197 .	and user datagram length, then inverted and if finally
  :                            198 .	zero inverted again. Then compared.
  :                            199 
  :                            200 	$if	RX_FRAGMENTS
  :                            201 udp_rx_rasm
  :                            202 	lx	descriptor:uproto
  :                            203 	la	uugram:checksum, x
  :                            204 	jza	udp_rx_rasm_nocheck
  :                            205 	push	a
  :                            206 	z	uugram:checksum, x
  :                            207 	lcal	rasm77_gcheck
  :                            208 	tnz	b		. because zero isn't
  :                            209 	xorB,xi	-1		. a UDP checksum
  :                            210 	xorB	0, sp
  :                            211 	pop	uugram:checksum, x
  :                            212 	la	fp
  :                            213 	jnzb	net_free_chain
  :                            214 udp_rx_rasm_nocheck
  :                            215 	la	fp
  :                            216 	lx, xi	udpq	
  :                            217 	j	net_add_chain
  :                            218 	$endif
  :                            219 
  :                            220 
  :                            221 .	call call vector udp_send gets to here
  :                            222 .	other external routines in this bank
  :                            223 .	can do a naughty jump here instead
  :                            224 .	of a far return
  :                            225 
  :                            226 udp_tx
00:0003E5 FF0DE2              +227 	call	(udp_txbuffer)
  :                            228 	.
  :                            229 	.
00:0003E6 B603F5              +230 	j	udp_txaw	. naughty jump
  :                            231 				. instead of	call	(udp_acw_send)
  :                            232 				.		fret	0
  :                            233 
  :                            234 
  :                            235 				. far call vector udp_txbuffer gets to here
  :                            236 
  :                            237 udp_buy_txaw
00:0003E7 6D4500              +238 	lb, xi	04500		. no option bytes, TOS default 0
  :                            239 
  :                            240 udp_buy_txaw_optional
00:0003E8 F80004              +241 	push	a
00:0003E9 C50008              +242 	aa, xi	8		. UDP header length
00:0003EA 3E0272              +243 	lcal	ip_buy_datagram
00:0003EB 380005              +244 	pop	b
00:0003EC C603F4              +245 	jza	udp_txbx
00:0003ED 28C003              +246 	sb	descriptor:bytes, a
00:0003EE 500013              +247 	lx	TX_DATAGRAM
00:0003EF 6D3C11              +248 	lb, xi	IP_DEFAULT_TTL++IPPROTO_UDP
00:0003F0 28A004              +249 	sb	dgram:ttl_protocol, x
00:0003F1 500012              +250 	lx	TP_HEADER
00:0003F2 54A004              +251 	lx, i	8/2, x
00:0003F3 10C006              +252 	sx	descriptor:apdata, a
  :                            253 udp_txbx
00:0003F4 3D0000              +254 	fret	0
  :                            255 				. this is a far call because
  :                            256 				. socket users may call it
  :                            257 
  :                            258 				. unlike the similar calls
  :                            259 				. in icmp and tcp which are
  :                            260 				. not application visible
  :                            261 
  :                            262 
  :                            263 
  :                            264 . far calls
  :                            265 
  :                            266 .	udp_acw_send
  :                            267 .	udp_acw_send_nocheck
  :                            268 
  :                            269 . get to here
  :                            270 
  :                            271 . udp_txaw
  :                            272 . udp_txaw_nocheck
  :                            273 
  :                            274 . low level call with the acw adress in X
  :                            275 
  :                            276 . both ports must be in the UDP header
  :                            277 . both adresses must be in the address buffer
  :                            278 
  :                            279 . if there is no come-from address in the address buffer
  :                            280 . the thing cannot now be checksummed and an earlier
  :                            281 . call should have done the internal routing step to
  :                            282 . find the sending interface and its default network
  :                            283 . address
  :                            284 
  :                            285 . so in that case an error gets returned as described
  :                            286 . in the rest of this discussion
  :                            287 
  :                            288 . it's the interface number that is important in
  :                            289 . finding a local address for the datagram and
  :                            290 . for the checksum
  :                            291 
  :                            292 . it's also optimal if the interface index is already
  :                            293 . in the descriptor
  :                            294 
  :                            295 . we have the local address and the interface number
  :                            296 . already when we just bounce back reversing the
  :                            297 . incoming addresses as a server with the calls to
  :                            298 
  :                            299 . 	udp_rtxbuffer->udp_response_buffer
  :                            300 
  :                            301 . and after message construction, to here:
  :                            302 
  :                            303 .	udp_acw_send->udp_txaw
  :                            304 
  :                            305 . some implementations suggest you do a connect
  :                            306 . which makes a routing call to IP and plugs the
  :                            307 . default network address of the interface back
  :                            308 . into the socket
  :                            309 
  :                            310 . having a from address in the socket is
  :                            311 . usable when you are making the conventional
  :                            312 . calls send and sendto
  :                            313 
  :                            314 . the connect idea is nice for send but not
  :                            315 . for sendto which is meant for random desinations
  :                            316 . reached over various interfaces
  :                            317 
  :                            318 . it's also not right that an application should
  :                            319 . need to pick a local network address before
  :                            320 . binding a UDP socket. The application and its
  :                            321 . developer cannot know that unless the
  :                            322 . application is for one host with one interface
  :                            323 
  :                            324 . since this is the final path step of all the
  :                            325 . possible interface calls to send UDP
  :                            326 
  :                            327 . and the step where checksum is done if opted
  :                            328 . and the come-from address therefore needed
  :                            329 
  :                            330 . we'll do the following here
  :                            331 
  :                            332 . we'll return with an error code if there is
  :                            333 . no source address, because
  :                            334 
  :                            335 . if you got here with the primitive
  :                            336 
  :                            337 .	udp_acw_send
  :                            338 
  :                            339 . then it was up to you to copy the address
  :                            340 . information into the buffers. At this
  :                            341 . point it's no longer certain that the
  :                            342 . socket/port structure is based. That's
  :                            343 . not even in the parameters to this call
  :                            344 . and it's intended that the caller decides
  :                            345 . what at this point is based in socket
  :                            346 . space
  :                            347 
  :                            348 . so we'll send back an error
  :                            349 
  :                            350 . if you obtained the buffer with
  :                            351 
  :                            352 .	udp_rtxbuffer
  :                            353 
  :                            354 . then that DID set the addresses up for you
  :                            355 
  :                            356 . if you came from send, send had access
  :                            357 . to your socket and copied the addresses
  :                            358 . in there
  :                            359 
  :                            360 . if you then get to here with no come-from
  :                            361 . address you hadn't done the connect call
  :                            362 . which writes both to and from addresses
  :                            363 . to the socket. So the same error return
  :                            364 . is appropriate, we'll call it something
  :                            365 . like
  :                            366 
  :                            367 . UDP_NOSOURCENET	$equ	-5
  :                            368 
  :                            369 . if you got here from sendto, that makes
  :                            370 . the same internal routing call as connect
  :                            371 . for every datagram
  :                            372 . but writes what it finds to the datagram
  :                            373 . address buffer and not to the socket
  :                            374 
  :                            375 . recvfrom can cheekily write all the
  :                            376 . incoming addressing to the socket
  :                            377 
  :                            378 . then sendto can skip the internal routing
  :                            379 . step if the socket has a source address
  :                            380 . and the destinaton address stored on the
  :                            381 . socket is the sendto parameter address
  :                            382 
  :                            383 . send can just send on the recvfrom
  :                            384 . addresses as if it had connected
  :                            385 
  :                            386 . since the routing call finds the next hop
  :                            387 . as well as the interface, that information
  :                            388 . stays in the address buffer of the datagram
  :                            389 . and IP routing will not need to find the
  :                            390 . next hop again for this one datagram
  :                            391 
  :                            392 . for connect/send the next-hop isn't saved,
  :                            393 . because it might change. But the interface
  :                            394 . is saved
  :                            395 
  :                            396 
  :                            397 
  :                            398 udp_txaw

  :                            653 udp_check_payload_tx
00:0004DD 3E04AA              +654 	lcal	check_payload_tx

  :                            658 
  :                            659 
  :                            660 .	atomic reads and writes of buffer queues shared between the IP task
  :                            661 .	and socket users
  :                            662 
  :                            663 .	the lock cells are in the socket structures, because not all queues need them
  :                            664 
  :                            665 .	some socket users are application protocols in the IP task and some are
  :                            666 .	different tasks
  :                            667 
  :                            668 .	udp_write_rxq is called from udp in the IP task
  :                            669 .	the socket structure is in x
  :                            670 
  :                            671 udp_write_rxq
  :                            672

00:0004E8 27F000              +676

  :                            678 
  :                            679 
  :                            680 .	udp_read_rxq is called by the socket user which may be a server protocol
  :                            681 .	in the IP task or it may be another task
  :                            682 
  :                            683 .	again, the socket structure pointer is in x
  :                            684

00:0004F4 350000              +695 	lret	0
  :                            696 
  :                            697 
  :                            698 .	it's been decided that
  :                            699 
  :                            700 
  :                            701 .	inward traffic will always be in rx_buffers
  :                            702 .	traffic on loopback will just be copied
  :                            703 
  :                            704 udp_drop_dgram

  :                            195 
  :                            196 
  :                            197 	.	0:	is the remote ACK possible?
  :                            198 
  :                            199 	.	0:5	is there a remote reset?
  :                            200 
  :                            201 	.	1:	cache remote ACK and window
  :                            202 
  :                            203 	.	2:1	send waiting segments as remote
  :                            204 	.		window allows
  :                            205 
  :                            206 	.	2:2	release segments queued for ACK
  :                            207 	.		according to remote ACK value
  :                            208 	.		vs sequence + size of sent output
  :                            209 
  :                            210 	.	3:	compare remote sequence with local
  :                            211 	.		ACK
  :                            212 
  :                            213 	.	4:	if less, shave the leading edge
  :                            214 	.		of the input
  :                            215 
  :                            216 	.	5:	if more but sequence + length
  :                            217 	.		within local ACK + window, deferq
  :                            218 	.		and otherwise drop
  :                            219 
  :                            220 	.	6:	accept as much of the net input at 4:
  :                            221 	.		as local window allowed. This avoids
  :                            222 	.		accepting single bytes which are
  :                            223 	.		sent to push the window open
  :                            224 	.		and also avoids rejecting payload
  :                            225 	.		for which we have advertised
  :                            226 	.		window. Drop the segment if no
  :                            227 	.		part of it is accepted, and shave
  :                            228 	.		the trailing edge if part of it
  :                            229 	.		is rejected
  :                            230 
  :                            231 	.		It might be unwise to accept beyond
  :                            232 	.		window. A well behaved remote system
  :                            233 	.		won't send beyond window (apart from
  :                            234 	.		experimental 1-byters, and the response
  :                            235 	.		to those is drop them and advertise
  :                            236 	.		new window). A malicious remote system
  :                            237 	.		might try to manipulate window
  :                            238 
  :                            239 	.	7:	Advance local ACK and reduce local
  :                            240 	.		window according to the amount accepted
  :                            241 
  :                            242 	.	8:	generate new local window
  :                            243 
  :                            244 	.	9:	transmit segments queued for remote window
  :                            245 	.		according to remote ACK + window,  and
  :                            246 	.		if there is none, and no further input
  :                            247 	.		segment after this has yet been received
  :                            248 	.		for this connection, send an ACK, unless
  :                            249 	.		the incoming segment is an ACK without
  :                            250 	.		payload and its SEQ is not beyond local ACK
  :                            251 
  :                            252 	.		SYN and FIN have a payload weight of
  :                            253 	.		1 byte
  :                            254 
  :                            255 	.		restart connection:timer(1)
  :                            256 	.		maybe do this at an early point, depending
  :                            257 	.		whether the attempt to communicate
  :                            258 	.		counts as keepalive even if it is
  :                            259 	.		out of range
  :                            260 
  :                            261 
  :                            262 
  :                            263 
  :                            264 	.	1	is the remote ACK possible?
  :                            265

  :                            813 		. The timeout is signalled upwards by connection state change
  :                            814 		.

00:0008DB 350000              +1910 	lret	0
  :                            1911 
  :                            1912 
  :                            1913 .	buffer queues which are shared between tasks have to be accessed
  :                            1914 .	in a protected way. They are mainly the queues which both the IP
  :                            1915 .	task and an application protocol access. Some application protocols
  :                            1916 .	are in the IP task and some are not
  :                            1917 
  :                            1918 .	it would be quite bad to place TS cells in every queue structure
  :                            1919 .	because not all queues need them. Sockets and connections are the
  :                            1920 .	best available place for these lock cells
  :                            1921 
  :                            1922 .	all this code is SMP, so the code
  :                            1923 
  :                            1924 .	1.	prevents itself from getting interrupted or switched
  :                            1925 .	2.	locks the connection
  :                            1926 .	3.	updates the queue
  :                            1927 .	4.	frees the lock
  :                            1928 .	5.	reloads the interrupt mask it had before
  :                            1929 
  :                            1930 
  :                            1931 .	tcp_write_rxq is called from TCP in the IP task
  :                            1932 
  :                            1933 tcp_write_rxq
  :                            1934

00:0008DD 27F000              +1935 
00:0008DE 07A003              +1935 
00:0008DF B608DE              +1935 
00:0008E0 64A010              +1936 	la, i	transmission:receiveq
(+01)10

  :                            41 
  :                            42 .	these blocking-only calls are central to the
  :                            43 .	known classical POSIX-like socket interface
  :                            44 
  :                            45 .	they correspond to the vectors	listen()
  :                            46 .					accept()
  :                            47 .					close()
  :                            48 .					connect()
  :                            49 
  :                            50 .	their blocking characteristic is in this file connect.msm
  :                            51 
  :                            52 .	and they complement		recv()
  :                            53 .	and				send()
  :                            54 
  :                            55 .	which block or not per flags argument
  :                            56 
  :                            57 .	first parameter of these routines is the handle integer
  :                            58 
  :                            59 .	the target connections of these routines are
  :                            60 .	in the default connections array
  :                            61 .	not the high-volume connection arrays
  :                            62 
  :                            63 
  :                            64

  :                            536 XDR_TCP_INLINE $set 0
  :                            537 	$if	XDR_TCP_INLINE
  :                            538 
  :                            539 .	there is no detailed guidance from IETF, SUN or anywhere else
  :                            540 .	whether marked record fragments can split fields up
  :                            541 
  :                            542 .	Apple's NFS / TCP is written as if that was possible, so this
  :                            543 .	is also written as if a new record mark could start in the
  :                            544 .	middle of an opaque or number field
  :                            545 
  :                            546 .	these XDR calls will only work on a blocking connection
  :                            547 .	so the socket owner may not be the IP stack itself for these
  :                            548

  :                            598 tcp_read_opaque
  :                            599 	push	fp
  :                            600 	z	WDATAC
  :                            601 			. ask for the number of bytes you actually want
  :                            602 			. round up the descriptor:readpointer
  :                            603 			. in the final buffer read
  :                            604 			. push out the last delivered word to 24 bits
  :                            605 
  :                            606 	push	a	. how much you want
  :                            607

  :                            611

  :                            615 	and, xi	3	. flag up not 4 multiple

  :                            619 	j	tcp_round_opaque_read

  :                            622 			. for all present, so if no
  :                            623 			. segment buffer is at the
  :                            624 			. queue head, we are scanning
  :                            625 			. the wrong structure

  :                            140 
  :                            141 .	now keep looking to see if the new heap top
  :                            142 .	buffer is on its free chain. If it is, it
  :                            143 .	is the one at the far end, because the
  :                            144 .	highest-positioned buffers are the last
  :                            145 .	choice for re-use
  :                            146 
  :                            147 .	the stack of descriptors grows down towards
  :                            148 .	the heap of buffers which grows up
  :                            149 
  :                            150 .	the stack of descriptors packs back up
  :                            151 .	away from the heap of buffers which
  :                            152 .	packs down
  :                            153 
  :                            154 
  :                            155

00:000C5B 60B000              +251 readq	la	queue:head
00:000C5C C60C5F              +252 	jza	readqz

00:000C5E 28B000              +254 	sb	queue:head
00:000C5F 350000              +255 readqz	lret	0
  :                            256 
  :                            257         $if      QSIMULTANEOUS
  :                            258 
  :                            259 .	descriptors are short-term on two chains
  :                            260 .	so that TCP segments awaiting ACK
  :                            261 .	can be on the interface queue and the
  :                            262 .	connection's ACK wait queue at the same time
  :                            263 
  :                            264 .	TCP uses chain link descriptor:next and so does
  :                            265 .	almost all the t77.4 protocol suite
  :                            266 
  :                            267 .	interface queues use chain link descriptor:ifql
  :                            268 
  :                            269 .	ifql is a reuse of descriptor:readpoint which
  :                            270 .	which points TCP past consumed / duplicate
  :                            271 .	payload in incoming segments
  :                            272 
  :                            273 .	in high-volume space where certain TCP ports
  :                            274 .	have private buffer pools [HVBANK], the buffer
  :                            275 .	and its descriptor are in the same bank
  :                            276 
  :                            277 .	the descriptor is always located by a chain
  :                            278 .	head or previous descriptor pointing forward
  :                            279 .	to it
  :                            280 
  :                            281 .	in the private buffer bank arrangement TCP
  :                            282 .	inbound segment descriptors are located with
  :                            283 .	the pointer previous:sobdi.previous:next
  :                            284 
  :                            285 .	descriptor:sobdi is part of a next pointer
  :                            286 .	and does not concern the buffer of the descriptor
  :                            287 
  :                            288 .	sobdi is a reuse of apdata, which points to
  :                            289 .	the trailing edge of tcp segment header /
  :                            290 .	leading edge of tcp segment payload. apdata
  :                            291 .	is only needed during consumption and
  :                            292 .	construction of datagrams, and in high-volume
  :                            293 .	private buffer arrangements is replaced
  :                            294 .	during input and output by sobdi, which is
  :                            295 .	the bank base of the socket's private buffer 
  :                            296 .	pool
  :                            297 
  :                            298 .	during operations within TCP the bank containing
  :                            299 .	the segment is in address space whenever the socket
  :                            300 .	is in address space	
  :                            301 
  :                            302 .	high-volume private buffer pools are only used
  :                            303 .	in some TCP traffic which is expected to be of
  :                            304 .	extreme volume or confidential. The private buffer
  :                            305 .	arrangement enables millions of transmitted segments
  :                            306 .	awaiting remote acknowledgement to remain buffered
  :                            307 
  :                            308 .	interface device drivers drop transmitted datagrams
  :                            309 .	from protocols which do not resend
  :                            310 
  :                            311 .	TCP flags bit 23 of descriptor:bytes and interface
  :                            312 .	drivers do not drop those buffers after transmission
  :                            313 
  :                            314 
00:000C60 70B000              +315 q2if	tz	queue:head
00:000C61 B60C66              +316 	j	q2ifz
00:000C62 20B000              +317 	sa	queue:head
00:000C63 20B001              +318 	sa	queue:tail
00:000C64 30C007              +319 	z	descriptor:ifql, a
00:000C65 350000              +320 	lret	0
  :                            321 
00:000C66 68B001              +322 q2ifz	lb	queue:tail
00:000C67 20D007              +323 	sa	descriptor:ifql, b
00:000C68 20B001              +324 	sa	queue:tail
00:000C69 30C007              +325 	z	descriptor:ifql, a
00:000C6A 350000              +326 	lret	0
  :                            327 
00:000C6B 60B000              +328 readqif	la	queue:head
00:000C6C C60C6F              +329 	jza	rqifz
00:000C6D 68C007              +330 	lb	descriptor:ifql, a
00:000C6E 28B000              +331 	sb	queue:head
00:000C6F 350000              +332 rqifz	lret	0
  :                            333 
  :                            334 
  :                            335 	$if	HVBANK
  :                            336 
  :                            337 .	these macros work in A$BUFFB which is TCP outgoing
  :                            338 .	segments private buffer window, and cannot be used
  :                            339 .	to manage any other memory window 
  :                            340 
  :                            341 .	the descriptor + buffer returned at hvreadifq are
  :                            342 .	placed in memory window A$BUFFB
  :                            343 
  :                            344 .	the descriptor + buffer queued by q2ifhv are not
  :                            345 .	afterwards guaranteed in the address space, but
  :                            346 .	their long address is still in a:b
  :                            347 
  :                            348 q2ifhv	tz	1, y
  :                            349 	j	q2ifhv_tail
  :                            350 	ds	hvqueue:head 
  :                            351 	ds	hvqueue:tail
  :                            352 	lret	0
  :                            353 q2ifhv_tail
  :                            354 	dpush	a
  :                            355 	dpush	a
  :                            356 	dl	hvqueue:tail		. get qtail word offset pointer
  :                            357 	sabr	A$BUFFB			. get *qtail in address space
  :                            358 	pop	descriptor:sobdi, b	. write new qtail bank pointer
  :                            359 	pop	descriptor:ifql,b	. write new qtail word offset pointer
  :                            360 	pop	a			. take 2nd copy descriptor pointer
  :                            361 	pop	b			. from the internal stack
  :                            362 	ds	hvqueue:tail
  :                            363 	lret	0
  :                            364 
  :                            365 hvreadqif
  :                            366 	dl	hvqueue:head
  :                            367 	jzb	hvreadqifz		. queue empty
  :                            368 	sabr	A$BUFFB			. first descriptor into address space
  :                            369 	push	descriptor:sobdi, b	. next pointer to queue head
  :                            370 	push	descriptor:ifql, b
  :                            371 	pop	1, y
  :                            372 	pop	0, y
  :                            373 hvreadqifz
  :                            374 	lret	0
  :                            375 
  :                            376 .	these macros use descriptor:next pointer like readq
  :                            377 .	and q2, and are used in upper protocols
  :                            378 
  :                            379 .	descriptor:sobdi is the containing bank pointer of
  :                            380 .	the descriptor + buffer at descriptor:next
  :                            381 
  :                            382 .	these macros work in A$BUFFA which is TCP incoming
  :                            383 .	segments private buffer window, and cannot be used
  :                            384 .	to manage any other memory window
  :                            385 
  :                            386 .	the descriptor + buffer returned from hvreadq are
  :                            387 .	based in window A$BUFFA
  :                            388 
  :                            389 .	the descriptor + buffer queued by q2hv are not
  :                            390 .	afterwards guaranteed in the address space
  :                            391 .	but their long address is still in a:b
  :                            392 
  :                            393 q2hv	tz	1, y
  :                            394 	j	q2hv_tail
  :                            395 	ds	hvqueue:head, y
  :                            396 	ds	hvqueue:tail, y
  :                            397 	lret	0
  :                            398 q2hv_tail
  :                            399 	dpush	a
  :                            400 	dpush	a
  :                            401 	dl	hvqueue:tail, y
  :                            402 	sabr	A$BUFFA
  :                            403 	pop	descriptor:sobdi, b
  :                            404 	pop	descriptor:next, b
  :                            405 	pop	a
  :                            406 	pop	b
  :                            407 	ds	hvqueue:tail, y
  :                            408 	lret	0
  :                            409 
  :                            410 hvreadq	dl	hvqueue:head, y
  :                            411 	jzb	hvreadqifz
  :                            412 	sabr	A$BUFFA
  :                            413 	push	descriptor:next_bdi, b
  :                            414 	push	descriptor:next, b
  :                            415 	pop	1, y
  :                            416 	pop	0, y
  :                            417 	lret	0
  :                            418 
  :                            419 
  :                            420 	$endif	HVBANK
  :                            421 	$endif	QSIMULTANEOUS
